family_audit.reason: This family just produced the new best custom run at 24.419329 ms and beat CUTLASS, so it remains a healthy exploit fallback but not the primary next branch for round 2/20.
Now that the PTX one-k branch is the accepted base, the biggest clean orthogonal headroom signal is still CTA geometry and block-count overhead on the hot band.
The winning PTX branch is worth one bounded in-family follow-up, but only after testing the larger geometry family first.
This remains the cheap locality probe if both the 256x128 pivot and a bounded PTX exploit pass stall out.
That family already recorded a measured loss in the immediately previous round.
Barrier stall is down to 5.41%, so reopening the stage-deepening path is still not the best next use of budget.
The old sweep-backed full-band route remains stale and previously regressed badly on this restored surface.

directions.name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
directions.hypothesis: Round 1/20 established the PTX one-k 128x128 branch as the new accepted base at 24.419329 ms, which already beats CUTLASS. That removes the pressure to keep shaving only the same control path and makes round 2/20 a better place to test the strongest orthogonal family still sitting in-tree. The hot-band PTX kernel is still the wall at 32.687232 ms with 200 registers per thread and only 16.62% active warps while launching a 60x50 grid over the first 6400 x 7680 rows. The existing 256x128 pivot family halves the pivot-band CTA count and restores an 8-warp CTA, so it is the cleanest next test of whether hot-band geometry and block-count overhead are now the limiting factor.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band CTA geometry and block-count overhead on the current 128x128 PTX base, rather than a remaining local control-path bubble inside the winning branch.
directions.risk: Moderate. The kernel already exists in-tree, but routing the default path onto it changes the hot-band family more materially than another PTX micro-retune and could lose if the larger CTA worsens the live set.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the new 24.419329 ms base
hot-band kernel name and grid size in ncu_details.csv
ranking_notes: This is now the highest-upside untouched family on top of the new PTX base.
It is intentionally ranked ahead of more PTX-local tuning because round 1/20 already validated the current family.
If this loses cleanly, the search can fall back to tighter exploit passes on the PTX base.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 pivot branch cannot beat the 24.419329 ms PTX base or if it lowers active warps / raises registers without improving hot-band time.

directions.name: Retune The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Hot-Band Control Path
directions.hypothesis: The PTX one-k branch just delivered a large win, which is evidence that this family is the right base even if it may still carry some local waste. The hot-band kernel still runs at 32.687232 ms with 200 registers per thread, 7.20% long-scoreboard stall, and 5.41% barrier stall. A bounded exploit pass inside the PTX family should focus only on the PTX accumulate/export and one-k handoff path, not on changing geometry again. That makes it the best fallback if the 256x128 pivot family fails to capitalize on its theoretical block-count advantage.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual control-path overhead and live-range pressure inside the winning PTX one-k 128x128 hot-band branch.
directions.risk: Moderate. This stays on the best-known family, but PTX microkernel changes are correctness-sensitive and the remaining upside is probably narrower than the geometry branch.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.419329 ms base
ranking_notes: Keep one bounded exploit branch alive because the PTX family is now the accepted winner.
This is ranked below 256x128 because the geometric branch is the stronger orthogonal test at the current state.
If the pivot family loses, this becomes the next likely best move.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a first PTX-local retune does not improve hot-band time or if it raises registers further without a runtime gain.

directions.name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 PTX Grid
directions.hypothesis: The winning PTX base still launches a large 60x50 hot-band grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can stay resident per SM. In that low-occupancy regime, a small grouped-row or serpentine traversal remap may recover some inter-CTA locality without changing the mathematical kernel, shared-memory footprint, or CTA size. This remains the cheap, low-surface fallback branch if both the 256x128 pivot and a bounded PTX exploit pass fail to move the frontier.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and traversal efficiency on the current 128x128 PTX hot-band grid.
directions.risk: Low to moderate. The surface is small, but previous CTA-order experiments on other hot-band families were mixed, so the upside is bounded.
ranking_notes: This stays alive because it is the cheapest reversible probe on the current best family.
It remains below the other two because the likely upside is smaller and the evidence is weaker.
Use it only if the larger geometry and PTX-local branches both flatten.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if CTA traversal changes leave hot-band time and cache-facing counters flat after one round.

notes: Round 2/20 human-review audit: the queue still contributes only the approval gate and the exactly-one-direction rule, with no extra user-authored family to prioritize. The just-finished round reopened the PTX one-k 128x128 control branch and turned it into the new accepted base at 24.419329 ms, which now beats the recorded CUTLASS baseline. Because that family already validated strongly, the best next use of the round budget is an orthogonal family test rather than another immediate local nibble on the same branch. Accepted as the primary family for this diagnosis is the existing 256x128 pivot hot-band kernel, because the hot-band wall is still 32.687232 ms with 200 registers per thread and only 16.62% active warps. Deferred fallback families are a bounded PTX-local exploit pass and a light traversal/locality retune on the current 128x128 PTX grid. Rejected again for this round are the standard one-k copy-cadence family, the two-K stage-deepening path, and the stale full-band 64x384 route.
